speaker: interviewee
text: Over the [INAUDIBLE]. Good. This is tape six, Phyllis Dreazen talking with Allen Caine. It's October 23, 1995 in Philadelphia. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: After all you went through, how did you manage to make a life, like what we would call a normal person?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war ended, I lived in Traunstein. I didn't have a normal life. A few months later, my brother got married. I was left over in their apartment by myself. I had nobody to be-- to shame myself. I just wanted to I-- to drown my sorrows, and I became an alcoholic. I drank like a fish. I had the wrong company. I had guys that were twice as old as I am. And it was easy to follow these things. I drank. The police already knew me. I didn't fight. I didn't-- I wasn't rough with anything. They used to pick me up, take me to my room, take my shoes off, put me to bed. In the morning, the next day, they used to come to me, I should give them a couple of cigarettes, with the German police. And that's how I lived. I hardly ate, just drank.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to this country, when I started driving a car, I didn't want to drink. I was afraid to drink anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's when I came back to civilization.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] All these experiences you had, how did they influence your marriage, having a stable relationship with a woman? And how did it influence your bringing up children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's one thing-- when the kids were little, I didn't want to tell them that-- that I would drink for four years. I didn't want to tell them that-- what I did, what kind of life I did. I used to fight with-- with the Nazis, Hungarian, because they couldn't go-- they weren't allowed to come in-- go home to their country. We used to fight with them, with knives, with guns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't give a damn what would happen to me. So, as a parent did you beat your kids the way you had been beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: God forbid, no. I disciplined them once in awhile, a little-- a little-- a little touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you scream at them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Call them names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [INAUDIBLE] you don't do that-- never, never. I know one thing, if my kid would-- God forbid-- would need a heart, I'd give them mine. And the same goes for my wife, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you were overprotective?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. Well, according to American people, we were. We were. But that's my business. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had the kids. They didn't ask to be brought up. You take care of them. It's not like my father did.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But now, working as much as you worked, when did you ever have time to see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- they know why I had to work hard. They know it. They know that we were-- we are poor. I have to sacrifice. You could speak with them. You could tell-- I could tell my son that he has to make the money as long as he can. You're going to go to college. And they cooperated. They were good. They were very good. [INAUDIBLE] for the time. We never had a babysitter for our kids. Either my wife or I stayed with them. We never went without our kids, no matter where-- always with our kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or your wife ever talk to them about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Neither you nor her?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they used to ask-- tell my wife, how come you have a mom, and Daddy doesn't have nobody? What does Daddy-- what did he do? What does he have on his arm? I-- I was afraid to talk things. I would break down in front of my children. They were young.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can I tell them was a-- what a concentration camp is? I'll tell them it's a jail. They'll say, what? My father in jail? For what? No matter what I did. So I kept quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they started to ask me questions. Then I answered them-- what's what. Also, only when they asked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went to school. They used to tell me, oh, I met so and so. He's a Holocaust survivor. I say, oh yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was that. I never talked to him more about it. And then, finally, when we got-- they got older, and I was always dreaming I would like to make the money that I can take my kids to Birkenau. It cost a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost three years ago. And it's the-- it was unbelievable. It was wonderful, it was wonderful-- my kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was good about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That my son comes over, he says, God, Dad. Dad, I never believed it. Look at that place, when he was in Birkenau. That size, that big? When they saw-- and my daughter, she's-- every little stone. Daddy, why is it so? Daddy, why is that like that? Every little bit she had to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you finally understood each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes. My son-- my son, he keeps it inside. He was once in-- in Munich, in Dachau. He-- I told him-- he went to England for business. I told him to stop at Dachau, he saw my name there in the museum. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he doesn't talk about it. He does not-- That's his type, he's the tough type, and Sharon, also not. But now-- now they-- they-- they know. They'll tell their friends, what's all about, what they-- what their father did. Why he was in the-- in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We're coming to an end. Is there anything that I haven't asked you that you'd like to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You know, in Golleshau, the good old days, we used to get together. Too many people-- weren't a lot-- four or five people. We used to pray to God, God, give me an hour to be-- it doesn't matter what shape, just give me-- let me go be free. Tell the people about everything. I never did think that I'll ever get married and have a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, there is a story. The poor man goes to the rabbi for the rabbi should give him a-- a [HEBREW]. So rabbi should bless him. What's his problem? He's blind. He's poor. And he's childless.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When he gets to the rabbi, the rabbi says to him, only one thing I can grant you, not all three of them. He says, good. Rabbi, I want you to bless me. I want to-- I would like to see my kids eat with silver spoons. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to see-- he was blind-- my kids-- he was childless. Who eats with silver spoons? Not a poor guy, only a rich. He goes out. He says, boy, I fooled a rabbi. I only asked for one, for all three of them. He asked me-- he told me he's going to give me a blessing on one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we fooled-- we did fool the enemy, but too late. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how I always end up with that saying. Do you understand me-- that little story? You go that little story?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I have a granddaughter what that's from a previous marriage-- my daughter-in-law. She's 8 years old. She's starting to ask questions about the number, but she doesn't know the word dying. She's 8 years old. But what do you then? Practically every time I go there, the little-- the little one-- 3 years old-- she walks-- she walks, comes back. Grandpop, what's that? I say, mama, that's nothing. That's nothing. She's 3 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm go on the bus, people look at me. I sit like that. I cover it up because I don't like people staring at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you haven't removed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't dare remove it. This doesn't do any good. They found out-- a friend of mine removed it in Detroit, where he lives. He removed it, and what do you think happened? You still have the dots. But they're not with ink. The dots are still there. And he is so sorry that he ever did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why would? I'm not ashamed of it. I didn't do any bad things for-- that I have that. You started out as a religious boy with peyos.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm having a problem. The sunlight's gone right down the lens, so I have to close the blinds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we running or off?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I can't continue to do it like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So let me ask you as we're finishing, you started out as a religious boy and now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now. I can't ask nobody that question. I asked smart people the question, why-- I'm not talking about myself-- why did it have to happen? They were all pure, good people. All the six million people-- they were pure, good people. Why did it happen to them? Why did they got killed? I used to-- I used to say my prayers in camp in Golleschau, in every other camp. I remember them. I used to say it. And later on, as the beatings continued heavier and heavier, I stopped saying the prayers, little by little.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, so many years later, that was my question-- do you say them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- yes. I wanted to bring my kids up, not fanatics-- bring them up a good heart that they are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. My kids, holidays, the high holidays, they fast. And my son-- he'll fight if [INAUDIBLE] somebody will call him a dirty Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: He has the size for it to fight too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's been a successful--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: --life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I can die in peace too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did my share.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're quite welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of the survivors of the Shoah and myself, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side, my father, the left, my father's brother, my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Aron Yitzchak. My uncle's name, [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, I think it's in the 1900s, in Poland, in my hometown Ciechanow. To my right, on the right side, is a Gentile. He's Catholic, a Polish Catholic. He always liked the Jewish customs. He went to Palestine at that time. Today it's Israel. In the middle, it's me. And on the left side is my brother, Chaim. He saved his jacket from the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is taken 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Late. In Traunstein in the officer's home where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Catholic guy in camp with you? In concentration--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with me. He was with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was, his last camp was also Lower Bavaria, a place called Placken. I always liked-- wanted to play soccer. We didn't have any uniforms at that time. That was in 1946, after the liberation. I was about 19 years old, full of pep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Germany, in Bavaria. And this-- our uniforms, we collected the money for the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recognize myself. So I was on my-- sitting on my knees, I think, in the front. As far as I know, I think there's a picture missing there of myself. And this was like when we came to this country, the name of the boat-- SS Marine Shark. We came from Bremen Hoffen. We landed in New York, picked up by the [INAUDIBLE] Jewish organization. And they took us out to the over-- other gate, which two uncles was-- were waiting for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was in-- The best looking couple I've ever seen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the right side is a good looking guy. That's me. On the left side is my better part, my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam is her name. We married in '52, February the ninth. Here we have our son, Arnold, his lovely wife, Diane, Rachel, the oldest daughter, and Marlee, their baby. Bless them all. In 1992, we took a trip to Poland [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as free people, of course. That's me on that-- over there on the right side. On the left side, my wife, and in the middle is a lady, a Catholic lady. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the prison camp she used to watch me every time she was outside. She used to see me going to work, sometimes coming home from work. She lived a few yards from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: From which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Golleschau camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he'll give you a sign when we start.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I say my name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. No. Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: To my right, our daughter, Sharon, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to her left, my wife Miriam.

speaker: interviewee
text: My right, but that's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharon is our Valentine's Day present. She was born February 14, 1958-- the best thing that could have happened to us. We love her very dearly, and we know that the love is reciprocated. And she means a great deal to us. We've been through a lot of hard and bad times, but also an awful lot of good times. And I think that that says it from my heart, as far as our daughter is concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sharon, what was it like growing up in a house with survivors for parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little different in comparison to my friends' lives. For one thing, I spoke Yiddish, so that was different. My father tells me I-- when I started speaking English I spoke with a Yiddish accent because I start speaking Yiddish before English. So that was different. I got to understand people that my friends couldn't understand. But the other thing that was very unusual for me is that I only knew one grandmother. I never had any other grandparents. And that was always different for me. And I knew that my parents lives were different. And I think because of it, we were brought up differently in terms of values and things that we-- we got. And you know, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about that a little.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just little things like, it sounds trivial, but I never got an allowance. And most of my friends got an allowance, and they had to do chores to get an allowance. And I never got one, but believe me I knew, and still know, the value of money. And it was-- if I needed something, I got it. If it was something trivial that I didn't necessarily need, maybe I'd get it, and maybe I wouldn't. Maybe it would be gift or whatever. But that was one little thing. There was always a closeness in the family. I remember, as a child of survivor, getting together with the [NON-ENGLISH] group which is was my-- all my father's [INAUDIBLE]. And we'd get together, whether it was a picnic on the 4th of July with the rest of them and my cousin. Or one thing I remember is Saturday nights they'd have a meeting, like every month, and they'd all get together. And when it was time for my brother and I to be up in bed, but you could always hear things going on. And invariably, stories would go back to things that happened in the concentration camp back in Poland. And one of the things that I guess I appreciated more as I grew up was that my parents didn't really hide things. And I knew it was difficult for them to talk about things, but we always heard the stories. And when they got together as a group, we heard the stories even more. I can't say I remember all the stories, but when I hear them again, they kind of ring a bell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you carry their pain? Did you feel responsible for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't feel responsible. But I don't know if it's a result of growing up as a child of survivors, but one thing that I remember as a child was if my parents were ever going somewhere together in the car or whatever, and I had an opportunity to go with them, I wanted to go with them. I was-- I think I was afraid of if, God forbid, something happened, I'd be left home by myself or with my brother, but still, that I wouldn't have my parents. And as I said, I don't know if that's a result of that. But I kind of think it was. I didn't feel responsible. And I've always admired both of them for it. Obviously not when I was real little, but as I got older, because I often wonder if I would've been able to survive the things that they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: The trip back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Well, the trip back to Poland-- my parents went in '85 and asked me if I wanted to go. At that time, they were going to Poland for a week, and that was it. They were coming home. They were going with my father's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what am I going to do in Poland for a week? I don't want to go to Poland. And I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think from the time they came back, I regretted it. I thought about it more and more. And I thought, they're here. My father, especially in terms of everything he went through, if I don't see it now with him, I'm not going to. And from that time on, I thought about it more and more. And when my uncle, my father's brother, was sick, and after he passed away, I remembered at the shiva house talking about it even more of with my cousins. And the subject had come up over the years. My parents came back from Poland in '85. They said they'd never go back again. And as time went on, we start talking about it. And my father told my cousins and I that if we wanted to go back, he would do it. And I knew it was going to be hard for him, but he would do it for us. And we started planning, and we went. And it was an unbelievable trip. And one of the things that we did was we went to Israel afterwards. And I think that really helped. But the trip itself was difficult. We had fun, believe it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was interesting though. I remember coming back from the trip and having people at work or other people I knew say, oh, how was your vacation? Did you have fun? Like, well, it wasn't that kind of trip? Or are you going back to see relatives? That was-- that was the best question. Like, yeah, right. I wish I had some relatives to see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But again, I knew it was difficult for both of my parents, my father especially. But it was very meaningful going back and seeing where he spent time, where he suffered, his hometown, his-- his house, his apartment that was now-- or it was a school, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: His elementary school, which was now an apartment building. And-- and the big river in Ciechanow, which turned out to be a big joke. It was one of the laughingstocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it a joke?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember the story, but my cousin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you ever see the guy caught a big fish like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousins remember my uncle telling them how big this river was, and they would go swimming and the water would be up to their ears.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never believed that they're going to see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got to the river, and the only way it would be up to your ears is if you stood on your head was my father's joke now. So I guess when you're in elementary school, and you see a little-- you know, the little desks-- they seem huge. And you go back 10 years later, and they're-- they're tiny. That was the same type of thing. But you know he showed us where he used to play soccer. And we-- he-- we cried a lot. All of us cried a lot. We laughed a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about the castle?

speaker: interviewee
text: The castle-- [INAUDIBLE]-- that's where we played soccer. And so we got to see a lot of things. And we got to see a lot of what Poland is now. And what it's become. And that was kind of difficult too. But it was definitely a worthwhile trip. And going back to Israel right after that was also very meaningful. And I had actually been there the year before, and I hadn't expected to go back. But I was glad we did. It was quite a contrast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make you closer as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it did. I think it did. I don't know about everybody else but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course it did. I think the children, both Sharon and Arnold-- Arnold doesn't speak very much about it, but his feelings are there. And it-- it-- I feel it drew us closer together. I always felt that we were a close family and showed concern for one another. But I think that this brought us even closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it actually brought my brother and I closer, at least temporarily, anyway. I mean, we're basically close, and we'd do anything for each other, but it was almost feeling a different kind of bond at that time, like, both of us knowing what he went through at the same time. And we actually got along pretty well, so that was nice. It was a plus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Allen, would you like the last word?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid to say anything. I'm afraid to say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Allen at this point is very emotional, and he's had a full day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I accomplished my dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you love them very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any last word?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm proud of both of them. It was the best Valentine's present I ever had too. So I came into the right family. That's all I can say. I couldn't ask for any better parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you all very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for sharing with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
